questions.question: What does coverage-guided fuzzing primarily use to prioritize new inputs?
options: Wall clock time
Execution coverage and interesting program behavior
Code style violations
Thread count
questions.explanation: Coverage-guided fuzzers keep or mutate inputs that explore new execution paths or trigger noteworthy outcomes.

questions.question: Why are sanitizers commonly paired with fuzzing?
options: To compress seed corpora
To make crashes deterministic
To expose memory and undefined-behavior defects as actionable failures
To avoid writing harnesses
questions.explanation: Sanitizers surface latent low-level bugs as explicit failures with traces, greatly improving fuzzing effectiveness.

questions.question: Which fuzz target is usually most effective?
options: A deterministic parser function with bounded side effects
An endpoint requiring live third-party APIs
A function dependent on system clock and network jitter
A test that writes to production DB
questions.explanation: Deterministic, side-effect-bounded targets maximize execution throughput and reproducibility, which are central for useful fuzz campaigns.

questions.question: What is the right next step after finding a crash reproducer?
options: Ignore unless user-visible
Minimize, fix root cause, and convert to regression test
Increase random seed count only
Delete the input to keep corpus clean
questions.explanation: A crash has value only if triaged and converted into durable protection; minimization and regression backfill prevent recurrence.

questions.question: When does structured fuzzing generally outperform pure byte mutation?
options: Never
When format-aware generation helps reach deeper semantic paths
Only for image files
Only in languages without memory safety
questions.explanation: Structured generators preserve syntactic validity so deeper parser and semantic logic can be exercised more consistently.
